text: This is Dr. Craig Rogers, Director of Renal Surgery at the Vatacuda Urology Institute at Henry Ford Hospital.
The surgeon unclamps the renal hilum after reconstructing the renal defect, allowing blood flow to resume through the renal vessels.
The robotic instrument elevates the kidney, allowing the surgeon to use the fourth arm prograsp instrument to remove the robotic bulldog clamps from the renal hilum.
The prograsp instrument securely grasps the notch of the robotic bulldog clamps, enabling the console surgeon to visually control and execute the removal of the clamps from the surgical field.
The surgeon removes the clamp from the renal vein, followed by the removal of the clamp on the renal artery, ensuring proper blood flow restoration.
After the clamps are removed from the renal hilum, the surgeon places additional sutures to further close the reconstructed defect in the kidney.
The surgeon uses Hemlock clips to cinch and tighten the closure of the renal defect, ensuring a secure seal.
The surgeon secures clips using laparotide or additional hemlock clips to prevent them from sliding back, ensuring stable closure of the defect.
The surgeon addresses bleeding by placing additional sutures and cinching hemlock clips to secure closure and control hemorrhage.
In conclusion, robotic partial nephrectomy is a safe and feasible procedure in selected cases even with multiple tumors.
Use of the fourth robotic arm especially in obese patients or patients with multiple tumors facilitates the procedure and gives a greater degree of autonomy to the surgeon.
